building:102024695.desc: Built under the Carian satrap Mausolus in the 4th century BC and enlarged in the 2nd century AD under Rome, this theater held 10,000-13,000 spectators; a nearby platform was once mistaken for a temple of Ares.

building:107080283.desc: A small Roman-era theater (likely an odeon for music and recitation) at Antipatris, the city Herod the Great rebuilt around 9 BCE; it may never have been finished or was heavily stripped of stone before excavation.

building:139971323.desc: Excavated in 1829-1830 at the Roman colony of Falerii Novi, this rock-cut theater (compared to the amphitheater at Sutri) yielded Augustan-era statues of Livia and her grandsons Gaius and Lucius Caesar; little survives above ground today.

building:150310592.desc: Discovered in 1985 at the Gallo-Roman vicus of Ricciacum, this 2nd-century AD theater seated about 3,500 and is notable for rare surviving 'honor seats' with backrests and armrests, unique among known theaters in Gaul.

building:150531239.desc: A 2nd-century BC theater roughly 165 feet in diameter at the Lycian city of Cyaneae (Kyaneai), considered the best-preserved structure at the site, which also has an extensive rock-cut necropolis.

building:151530992.desc: A late-3rd/2nd-century BC theater at the foot of the acropolis of Iuvanum, a Samnite-then-Roman town in Abruzzo; only the lowest seven rows of seating and part of the orchestra are still visible.

building:154948553.desc: The rock-cut cavea of a theater, roughly 115 meters across, was identified in 1997 on the Zegreg Tepe hillside at Tavium, capital of the Galatian Trocmi tribe and a Roman-era crossroads town.

building:177668735.desc: Small deme theater at Ikarion, cult center of the Rural Dionysia and reputed home of Thespis, traditionally credited with inventing tragedy; excavated in 1888-89 by the American School, revealing stone proedria seats beside the orchestra.

building:184642281.desc: A roughly 2,000-seat theater built for the Pausilypon villa of the Roman knight Vedius Pollio near Naples; on his death in 15 BC the estate passed to Augustus and remained an imperial retreat for his successors.

building:199686145.desc: Roman theater of the imperial-era colony of Privernum (modern Priverno), part of a marble-decorated public complex with statues and portraits of the imperial family; known mainly from excavation and epigraphic finds rather than literary sources.

building:204612618.desc: Roman-period theater at Tios (Tieion) on the Black Sea coast, judged in the late 19th century among the best-preserved in Asia Minor with an estimated capacity of about 7,000; quarried for building stone before excavations began in 2006.

building:221152517.desc: Small Roman theater at Adraha (modern Daraa, Syria), part of a classical quarter south of the Ottoman-era town alongside a colonnaded decumanus and a temple; known chiefly from surface ruins rather than formal excavation.

building:246534016.desc: Roman theater of Forum Iulii (Fréjus), rebuilt in stone under Tiberius on the site of an earlier wooden theater, with an original capacity of about 2,000; restored and returned to performance use in 1997.

building:248782117.desc: One of five known theaters at Cyrene, on the acropolis ridge; a minor, conventionally numbered structure distinct from the much larger Greek Theatre (Theatre 1) near the Sanctuary of Apollo, with little published individual documentation.

building:24962619.desc: Well-preserved 2nd-3rd century Roman theater at Thubursicum Numidarum (Khamissa, Algeria), considered one of the finest in North Africa, with a 56.8 m cavea and 43.6 m stage front; first excavated by French scholars, 1900-1922.

building:285468794.desc: Roman theater at the coastal colony of Castrum Novum, built in the Imperial age facing the sea, with a ~30 m stage and seating for roughly 1,000; brick stamps of Marcus Aurelius indicate 2nd-century restructuring, confirmed in excavations resumed since 2010.

building:293222275.desc: Mud-brick Greek theater built for Babylon's Greek community, probably dating to Alexander the Great's reign and rebuilt in the 2nd century AD per a Greek repair inscription; excavated by Robert Koldewey, final diameter about 24.8 m.

building:295344387.desc: A circular assembly hall (ekklesiasterion) unique in the Greek world, dominating Metapontum's agora and seating up to 7,500-8,000; converted into a theater around 325-300 BC, later repaired, then sealed up as a fortress.

building:339848370.desc: Roman-era odeon/small theater at ancient Kelenderis (Aydıncık), first traced from a surviving semicircular diazoma wall; excavations from 1989 and documentation since 2020 have exposed its cavea, orchestra, and stage.

building:364392645.desc: Monumental Gallo-Roman theater built around 120 AD east of Samarobriva (Amiens), with a 132 m facade ranking among Gaul's largest and estimated capacity over 5,000; unexpectedly discovered in 2006 excavations.

building:368305524.desc: A circular orchestra in the Athenian Agora used for dramatic and musical contests before the Theatre of Dionysus existed; a replacement set of markers was made by sculptors Kritios and Nesiotes in 476 BC.

building:372021190.desc: Stone-built theater of Carthaea (Karthaia) on Kea, dated to the 4th century BC and seating nearly 1,000; first examined by Danish antiquarian Peter Oluf Brønsted in 1812, later excavated and restored 2011-2015.

building:427981474.desc: Roman theater of Augusta Bagiennorum (Bene Vagienna), 1st century AD, with a cavea about 57 m across holding ~3,000 spectators; decorated with Luni marble and imported colored marbles, identified in the late 19th century and restored in the 1950s.

building:436809109.desc: Theater of the Roman colony Colonia Genetiva Iulia (Urso), founded by Julius Caesar for army veterans in 44 BC; its cavea rests against a natural hillside and has been documented by scholars since the 1870s.

building:448119705.desc: Theater of the Roman municipium of Singilia Barba near Antequera, with a cavea about 52 m across; known since the 16th century, its upper seating tiers were later quarried to build Antequera's Church of San Juan de Dios.

building:458747378.desc: Theater of ancient Telmessos (modern Fethiye), begun in the late Hellenistic or Augustan period with its stage added around 200 AD; excavated 1992-95, it held about 6,000 spectators across 28 rows.

building:48637573.desc: Roman theater at ancient Soli, Cyprus, dated by coin finds to the mid-1st century AD and used into the 4th century; excavated in 1927 by the Swedish Cyprus Expedition under Alfred Westholm and rebuilt in 1962-64, it seated about 3,500.

building:511519022.desc: Theater at Noviodunum Diablintum (Jublains), built over an earlier structure probably in the later 1st century AD; unusually elliptical and lacking a stage wall, it was donated to the town by the decurion Orgetorix per a surviving inscription.

building:523291014.desc: Theater of ancient Rhodiapolis in Lycia, with 16 surviving rows of seating; it stood before the funerary monument of Opramoas, the city's wealthy 2nd-century AD benefactor, and was fully excavated after 2006.

building:544710174.desc: Theater of the mountaintop Pisidian city Termessos, built in the Hellenistic 2nd century BC and remodeled by the Romans in the 2nd century AD; its diazoma-divided cavea seated 4,000-5,000, with animal pens beneath the stage for beast shows.

building:55405152.desc: Theater of ancient Tralles (Aydin), birthplace of Anthemius, architect of Hagia Sophia; the ruins, now on private farmland, are noted for the storks nesting atop them.

building:575107588.desc: Greek theatre built in the late 3rd century BC under Hiero II of Syracuse, with nine wedge-shaped seating sections and twelve rows holding roughly 700 spectators; still hosts performances today.

building:585690561.desc: Built under Augustus (late 1st century BC), Mediolanum's theatre had a 95m-wide cavea and 20m stage, holding an estimated 7,000-9,000 spectators; its remains lie beneath the Palazzo Turati.

building:612060178.desc: A 2nd-century BC theatre, among the last built in Greek style in Calabria, with a freestanding stone cavea originally holding around 1,200 spectators; discovered in 1883, fully excavated by 1925.

building:622079673.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Phoinike in Epirus, built mid-3rd century BC and renovated in the 2nd, seating roughly 7,000 with a 30m scaenae frons; remained in use after Rome annexed Epirus under Augustus.

building:625262900.desc: Built by Tigranes the Great around 69 BC for Greek drama performed by artists gathered from across his empire — the first known theatre in Armenia; still unfinished when Lucullus took the city and celebrated his triumph there.

building:69311541.desc: Roman theatre at Aquinum on the Via Latina in the Liri valley, birthplace of the poet Juvenal; excavations exposed 22 radial cavea walls in reticulate-faced concrete plus part of the stage and orchestra steps.

building:694724623.desc: Chance find in 1929 in the gardens of Palazzo Romano; built in the Augustan period, its six-wedge cavea (75m diameter) held some 5,000-6,000 spectators for comedies and tragedies, with later Antonine-era statuary added.

building:697765430.desc: Carved into a hillside outside Cuicul's walls, this two-tier theater seated about 3,000; a surviving inscription naming patrons Caius Julius Crescens and Caius Julius Didius Crescentianus dates its dedication to AD 161.

building:702050141.desc: Excavated in 1967 south of the Gosbecks Romano-Celtic temple near Camulodunum; an earthwork structure rebuilt in stone-revetted turf and clay, estimated to have seated up to 5,000 - the largest of Britain's five known Roman theaters.

building:702770983.desc: Theater of Roman Albintimilium, built roughly late 2nd-early 3rd century AD with its cavea partly cut into bedrock, holding about 2,000 spectators; systematically excavated from the 1930s onward.

building:713959974.desc: A Greek theater at Parium on the Hellespont, rebuilt by the Romans in the 2nd and early 3rd centuries AD; the site has been under renewed archaeological excavation since 2005.

building:716466643.desc: The oldest theater in Roman Africa, dedicated in AD 1-2 and partly financed by local benefactor Annobal Tapapius Rufus (who also built the city market); cut into a hillside once used as a cemetery, second in size in Africa only to Sabratha's theater.

building:717575054.desc: One of two theaters on the Mont Châtelet site near Breteuil (Oise); this smaller theater dates to the 1st century AD, while the adjacent grand theater (late 1st-early 2nd century) held about 4,000. Located via 1955 aerial photography and excavated 1956-1986.

building:727686421.desc: A well-preserved theater on the slopes of ancient Arykanda in Lycia, built around the mid-1st century BC with 20 rows of seats in seven wedges; its stage building was remodeled in the late 2nd-early 3rd century AD.

building:756231346.desc: The Theatre of Balbus, Rome's third public theatre, was built in 13 BC by Lucius Cornelius Balbus the Younger from spoils of his African campaign; its rear portico, excavated from 1981, gives the site its modern name Crypta Balbi.

building:763220261.desc: Hellenistic theatre (2nd century BC, with Roman-era alterations) at Laodicea on the Lycus, cut directly into bedrock and seating roughly 8,000-10,000; damaged by earthquakes in the 3rd century and in 368 AD, it was excavated and restored 2003-2021.

building:786052705.desc: Theatre of Stratos, capital of the Acarnanian League, begun in the late 4th century BC as a stoa-like stage building; enlarged through three phases into the 2nd century BC, it held some 6,000-7,000 spectators and is the largest theatre known in Acarnania.

building:832363479.desc: Built at the sacred festival site of Dion probably under Philip V (221-179 BC), this Hellenistic theatre hosted the Macedonian kings' Olympia games; tradition holds that Euripides's Archelaus and Bacchae, written during his stay at the Macedonian court, were staged there.

building:836258799.desc: Hellenistic theatre at Magarsus, the sea-facing sanctuary port of Mallos in Cilicia where Alexander the Great reportedly sacrificed to Athena Magarsis before Issus; excavated from 2013, it seated about 3,000 and is noted as the only ancient theatre with a sea view.

building:856482651.desc: Roman theatre of ancient Hatria, built in the 1st century AD under Augustus with a cavea about 70m across and capacity near 10,000; its remains were rediscovered in 1993 during demolition of a former orphanage building.

building:865005128.desc: Theatre of Gallo-Roman Decempagi, revealed only by aerial photography in 1981; an unusually large flat-ground cavea roughly 117m in diameter, estimated to have held up to 16,000, forming part of a major sanctuary complex at the site.

building:891801761.desc: Not a theater: this is the open courtyard (about 30x40m) northwest of the Minoan palace at Malia, which substituted for a formal theatral area since the flat terrain precluded one; its perimeter foundations once carried tiered seating for spectators.

building:899795549.desc: Known as the South Theater, this odeon at Hadrian's Villa seated about 1,100 in a 55m marble-and-mosaic cavea; connected to the rest of the imperial complex by underground service tunnels, built under Hadrian (r. 117-138).

building:932787931.desc: Built in the late 2nd century BC and enlarged under Rome to seat about 3,500, Kourion's theater was converted into a beast-fighting arena in the early 3rd century AD; excavated from 1933 by the University of Pennsylvania.

building:958299306.desc: A rock-cut theater built into the hillside at Stymphalos, a planned Classical Arcadian city excavated since 1982 and the legendary site of Herakles' sixth labor, the Stymphalian birds.

building:982593692.desc: Civic sanctuary of the Attic deme Ikarion, birthplace of tragedian Thespis and host of the winter Rural Dionysia; its theater, choregic monument, and temple site were excavated by the American School from 1888-89.

building:983828927.desc: Hellenistic theater at Alinda in Caria, built early 2nd century BC into the acropolis hillside with 35 rows seating roughly 5,000 spectators; renovated under Augustus.

place:dare-20255.desc: The Théâtre romain des Câteliers near Les Andelys (Noyers-sur-Andelys, Eure), discovered by Léon Coutil in 1927; at 120m diameter it ranks among the ten largest theaters of Roman Gaul, built 1st-2nd century AD.

place:pl-177540.desc: Ancient name of the Gallo-Roman town at Entrains-sur-Nohain, attested by a marble itinerary plaque found at Autun in 1840; aerial survey in 1974 revealed a theater about 115m across, among Gaul's largest.

vici:vici-10015.desc: Roman theater on a Treverian sanctuary plateau above the Saar near Kastel-Staadt, excavated 2006-2008; had 36 rows of seating for about 3,500 spectators, rebuilt in stone around 300 AD after an earlier timber phase.

vici:vici-10173.desc: Roman circus of Vienne, built at the end of the 2nd century AD for chariot racing along a roughly 460m track; its spina was marked by 'La Pyramide,' a 23m truncated obelisk on an arched base still standing today.

vici:vici-10202.desc: Roman theater of Aventicum (Avenches), built early in the 2nd century AD with capacity for roughly 11,000 spectators across about 40 rows; converted into a fortified refuge with a ditch in the later 3rd century.

vici:vici-10378.desc: Wooden theater of Nida, Roman civitas capital at Frankfurt-Heddernheim, its seating raised on an earthen bank; excavated in 1928 but now completely destroyed by later urban development.

vici:vici-10448.desc: Theater of Lopodunum, capital of the Civitas Ulpia Sueborum Nicrensium after 98 AD; inscribed stone seats naming individual donors and owners survive, now held in museums including the Badisches Landesmuseum Karlsruhe.

vici:vici-10517.desc: Amphitheater of Marcianopolis (Devnya, Bulgaria), excavated 1958-61 by Goranka Toncheva; measured about 65 by 58m with 12 rows of seating in 14 sections and two arched gates, including the porta libitinensis.

vici:vici-10518.desc: Amphitheater of Gorsium (Tác), the Pannonian provincial town that served as center of the imperial cult and, after rebuilding as Herculia, provincial capital; accommodated up to about 2,500 spectators.

vici:vici-10525.desc: Amphitheatre of Brigetio, a Danube-frontier legionary and civilian settlement (Komárom-Szőny, Hungary) that became a municipium around 205 AD; a 2009 epigraphic study argues the town had two separate amphitheatres, military and civic.

vici:vici-10604.desc: Roman theatre built under Augustus c. 40-30 BC, one of the earliest stone theatres in Gaul, dedicated to Apollo; seated roughly 8,000 and yielded the Venus of Arles, found in pieces in 1651.

vici:vici-10608.desc: Roman circus of Arles, built in 149 AD on the Rhône bank on thousands of wooden piles; 450m long with tiered seating for up to 20,000, used for chariot races. Its spina obelisk now stands in Arles' Place de la République.

vici:vici-10638.desc: Theatre of Gisacum, the vast Gallo-Roman sanctuary town near Évreux; among the largest in Roman Gaul at c. 106m diameter, holding several thousand spectators, its entrance aligned on the adjoining temple's axis.

vici:vici-10693.desc: Gallo-Roman arena-theatre at Thénac ('Les Arènes'), near Saintes; likely built under Nero and abandoned in the 3rd century, cavea roughly 84-90m across, part of a rural sanctuary with baths and a sacred spring.

vici:vici-10762.desc: Theatre of Lugdunum Convenarum (Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges), the Roman town traditionally linked to Pompey's 72 BC foundation; its cavea was remodelled around the mid-1st century AD with a large porticus post scaenam.

vici:vici-10866.desc: Hippodrome (circus) of Antinoöpolis, the city Hadrian founded in 130 AD to honour his drowned favourite Antinous; roughly 307m by 77m, it hosted the Antinoeia games of chariot racing and athletic contests.

vici:vici-10904.desc: The so-called 'Tomba di Agrippina' at Bauli (Bacoli), long linked in legend to Nero's mother but actually a private theatre-nymphaeum (teatro-ninfeo): an Augustan-era odeion within a seaside villa, later converted into a fountain hall.

vici:vici-10906.desc: Theatre remains on the citadel mound of ancient Beroea (Seleucid refoundation of an older Aramaean settlement), later renamed Aleppo; Roman-period Beroea had a colonnaded east-west avenue and a monumental triple arch, with the citadel serving as its acropolis.

vici:vici-10918.desc: Theatre-temple complex of the Samnite Pentri federal sanctuary at Pietrabbondante (identified in older scholarship with the town Bovianum Vetus), built in the late 2nd century BC with its cavea cut into the hillside below the larger Temple B.

vici:vici-10939.desc: Hierapolis Bambyce (modern Manbij, Syria), hellenized from the Aramaic Manbug and known to Greeks as the 'City of the Sanctuary' for its shrine of the Syrian goddess Atargatis, famously described in Lucian's De Dea Syria; distinct from the Phrygian Hierapolis.

vici:vici-10943.desc: Hellenistic theatre of the Carian port city Kaunos near modern Dalyan, seating roughly 5,000 across nine wedges of 33 rows with eight stairways, overlooking the city's silted harbor.

vici:vici-10959.desc: Lower theatre of Cnidus (Knidos) in Caria, built into the hillside beside a temple of Dionysus and holding an estimated 5,000-6,400 spectators; first surveyed by the Society of Dilettanti's 1812 mission and excavated by Charles Newton in 1857-58.

vici:vici-11050.desc: Roman-era (2nd-century AD) theatre of Gythion, Sparta's ancient port on the Laconian Gulf; excavated from 1891 by Andreas Skias, its marble cavea held twelve rows in five wedges with a front row of backed seats reserved for officials.

vici:vici-11094.desc: Theatre of Perinthus, the Samian colony on the Propontis renamed Heraclea under Diocletian; excavations at modern Marmara Ereğlisi have uncovered the theatre together with public baths and the city's fortification walls.

vici:vici-11100.desc: Theatre of Hierapolis (Phrygia), first built in the 2nd century BC, wrecked by the earthquake of 60 AD, then rebuilt under the Flavians in works lasting until c. 206 AD; among Anatolia's largest theatres, with an estimated capacity of 10,000-12,500.

vici:vici-11143.desc: A Hellenistic theatre cut into the hillside at Limyra in Lycia, built in the 2nd-1st century BC with a horseshoe cavea holding about 8,000; badly damaged in the 141 AD earthquake and rebuilt with funds from the Lycian benefactor Opramoas of Rhodiapolis.

vici:vici-11149.desc: The Ancient Theatre of Ohrid, built in Hellenistic style at the end of the 3rd or start of the 2nd century BC, the only theatre of its kind in North Macedonia; Romans later used it for gladiatorial games and it was a site of Christian executions under Diocletian.

vici:vici-11218.desc: Built at the end of the 1st century BC under Augustus near the forum and port of Tarraco, seating about 6,000 spectators over three tiers; part of the UNESCO-listed Archaeological Ensemble of Tárraco.

vici:vici-11241.desc: Spain's smallest surviving Roman theatre, carved directly into the bedrock at Pollentia (Alcúdia, Mallorca) in the 1st century AD to seat 1,500-2,000; excavated from 1952 and later reused as a late-Roman burial ground.

vici:vici-11249.desc: Hellenistic theatre of Mytilene on Lesbos (3rd-1st century BC), its cavea over 100m across; Pompey had its design copied to build Rome's first permanent stone theatre, dedicated in 55 BC.

vici:vici-11259.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman theatre at Neapolis (Samaria, modern Nablus), one of the largest in Roman Syria-Palestine with seating for several thousand; discovered by chance in 1978 during water-canal excavations, later converted into a water basin.

vici:vici-11262.desc: Built under Augustus in the early 1st century AD as part of Nicopolis's Actian Games complex commemorating his victory at Actium, with a 92m-diameter cavea seating roughly 5,000; in continuous use into the 3rd century AD.

vici:vici-11319.desc: Small basalt-built theatre (capacity around 2,000) at Philippopolis, the Hauran village emperor Philip the Arab (r. 244-249) rebuilt as a colonia; construction halted, unfinished, at his death.

vici:vici-11414.desc: Ancient Greek theatre of Thasos, cut into the acropolis hillside; first built early 3rd century BC with a marble stage front dedicated to Dionysus by Lysistratos, later used from the 1st century AD for beast hunts and gladiatorial combat.

vici:vici-11441.desc: Best-preserved structure at Tusculum: a Roman theatre with a 45m cavea and 17.5m orchestra, built early 1st century AD and excavated by Luigi Canina in 1839-40 for Lucien Bonaparte's campaign.

vici:vici-11444.desc: 2nd-century AD Roman hippodrome at Tyre, 480m by 90m and seating 20,000, among the largest and best-preserved in the Roman world; hosted the Heraclia games for Melqart-Heracles every four years.

vici:vici-11449.desc: Roman odeon on the Fourvière hill in Lyon (Lugdunum), built early-mid 2nd century AD with a 73m diameter seating 3,000; one of only two paired odeon-theatre complexes in Roman Gaul, alongside Vienne.

vici:vici-11462.desc: Gallo-Roman theatre at Vieux-Poitiers (ancient Vetus Pictavis, Naintré), 116m in diameter and seating an estimated 10,000 - larger than the theatre of Orange - hosting drama and gladiatorial combat for two centuries.

vici:vici-11463.desc: Theatre of the Roman vicus Augusti (modern Sidi el-Hani, Tunisia), with a 58m cavea and 22m orchestra; its ruins, with radial vaulted substructures, were first studied by Saladin and Cagnat in 1882.

vici:vici-11524.desc: Stone Roman theatre built AD 161 on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis by Herodes Atticus in memory of his wife Aspasia Annia Regilla; seated about 5,000 and still hosts the Athens Festival today.

vici:vici-11525.desc: Athens's original odeon, built 435 BC by Pericles for the musical contests of the Panathenaea, its square timber roof said to imitate Xerxes's tent; burned during Sulla's siege in 86 BC and later rebuilt.
